title: Quiz: Quantum RL Applications
description: Test your understanding of practical applications of quantum reinforcement learning

questions.question: In pulse-level quantum control, what does the RL agent typically learn?

options.text: Time-dependent control amplitudes (pulse shapes) to implement target operations

questions.explanation: In pulse control, the RL agent learns optimal time-dependent control fields (like Ω_x(t), Ω_y(t)) that implement desired quantum gates or state preparations.

questions.question: Why is circuit depth important in circuit optimization applications?

options.text: Deeper circuits are always more expressive
Shorter circuits have less accumulated error and faster execution
Depth determines the number of qubits
Depth is only important for simulation

questions.explanation: On real quantum hardware, each gate introduces errors and takes time. Shorter circuits minimize accumulated errors and decoherence effects.

questions.question: What is Adaptive VQE?

options.text: A VQE that uses fixed circuit depth
A variational approach where the circuit structure is learned alongside parameters
A VQE that only works for molecules

questions.explanation: Adaptive VQE learns which gates to add to the ansatz based on gradient information, potentially discovering more efficient problem-specific circuits.

questions.question: In QRL for MaxCut, what does the quantum state encode?

options.text: A superposition of bit strings representing possible vertex partitions

questions.explanation: The quantum state encodes a superposition over all possible bit strings, where each bit represents which partition a vertex belongs to. Measurement samples potential solutions.

questions.question: Including an energy penalty in pulse control rewards helps find low-power solutions that are more hardware-friendly.
questions.explanation: True. Penalizing high pulse amplitudes encourages the agent to find solutions that use less power, reducing heating and other hardware issues.

questions.question: What is syndrome decoding in quantum error correction?

options.text: Using parity check measurements to infer error locations without measuring data qubits

questions.explanation: Syndrome measurements check parity between pairs of qubits, revealing error patterns without collapsing the encoded logical information.

questions.question: What is the approximation ratio in combinatorial optimization?

options.text: The number of qubits divided by problem size
The achieved solution value divided by the optimal value
The circuit depth divided by the maximum allowed

questions.explanation: The approximation ratio measures solution quality: r = solution/optimal. A ratio of 1.0 means the optimal solution was found.

questions.question: Which metrics are important for benchmarking QRL applications? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: All are important: fidelity measures accuracy, sample efficiency shows learning speed, depth relates to hardware feasibility, and approximation ratio quantifies optimization quality.
